phase1.explanation:
FXML separates UI design from application logic:

--- What is FXML? ---

FXML is an XML-based language for defining JavaFX UIs:
• Declarative UI design
• Separates design from code
• Easier to maintain
• Visual design with Scene Builder

--- Benefits of FXML ---

1. Separation of Concerns:
   • UI in FXML files (.fxml)
   • Logic in Java controllers

2. Visual Design:
   • Scene Builder — drag-and-drop UI builder
   • No coding for basic layouts
   • WYSIWYG (What You See Is What You Get)

3. Maintainability:
   • Easier to modify UI
   • Designers can work on FXML
   • Developers work on controllers

4. Reusability:
   • Can include FXML files in others
   • Custom components

--- FXML Structure ---

Basic FXML:
```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>

<?import javafx.scene.control.*?>
<?import javafx.scene.layout.*?>

<VBox xmlns="http://javafx.com/javafx/17"
      xmlns:fx="http://javafx.com/fxml/1"
      alignment="CENTER" spacing="15">

    <Label text="Hello, FXML!" />
    <Button text="Click Me" />

</VBox>
```

--- Loading FXML ---

In your Java code:
```java
FXMLLoader loader = new FXMLLoader(getClass().getResource("example.fxml"));
Parent root = loader.load();
Scene scene = new Scene(root, 400, 300);
```

--- Controller Class ---

The controller handles UI logic:

```java
public class ExampleController {
    @FXML
    private Label label;
    
    @FXML
    private Button button;
    
    @FXML
    private void handleButtonClick() {
        label.setText("Button clicked!");
    }
}
```

--- @FXML Annotation ---

@FXML marks fields and methods for FXML injection:
• Fields: binds to FXML elements
• Methods: event handlers

--- Scene Builder ---

Scene Builder is a visual tool for creating FXML:
• Drag and drop components
• Set properties and styling
• Generate FXML code
• Preview UI

Benefits of Scene Builder:
• Faster UI development
• Visual feedback
• No need to memorize syntax

--- Workflow ---

1. Design UI in Scene Builder
2. Save as .fxml file
3. Create controller class
4. Load FXML in Java application
5. @FXML binds UI elements to controller
6. Add event handlers

--- When to Use FXML ---

Use FXML for:
• Complex UIs
• Team projects (designers + developers)
• When you want visual design

Use Java code for:
• Simple UIs
• Dynamic UI generation
• Programmatic layouts

FXML is the recommended approach for professional JavaFX applications!

phase2.explanation:
Conceptual lesson — no broken code!

Common misconception: "FXML is slower than writing Java code."

Actually: FXML is parsed once and has minimal overhead. The performance difference is negligible for most applications.

Another misconception: "I need Scene Builder to use FXML."

Actually: You can write FXML by hand in any text editor! Scene Builder is optional but helpful.

Another misconception: "FXML is just like HTML."

Actually: FXML is similar to HTML but has different tags and is specific to JavaFX. It's XML-based, not HTML.

phase3.prompt:
Which statement about FXML is TRUE?

A) FXML files are written in HTML
B) The @FXML annotation is used to bind UI elements to controller fields
C) FXML can only be created with Scene Builder
D) FXML makes JavaFX applications slower

Select the CORRECT statement.
dialogueHints: Think about how FXML connects to Java code...
Can you write FXML without Scene Builder?
